text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Disease Status: Metastatic Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma, Progressive Disease on First-Line Immunotherapy

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV clear cell RCC (with sarcomatoid differentiation) who presents today for urgent follow-up following restaging CT imaging demonstrating significant progression of her pulmonary and nodal disease. She endorses worsening shortness of breath and a new dry cough over the past several weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo initially presented with a large right renal mass identified incidentally, ultimately staged as T3b N2 M1 clear cell RCC with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. She underwent open right radical nephrectomy with regional lymph node dissection approximately 9 months ago, followed by initiation of first-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, then transitioned to nivolumab 480 mg IV flat dose every 4 weeks as maintenance. The rationale for dual checkpoint blockade included her intermediate IMDC risk classification, sarcomatoid histology (which carries a particularly poor prognosis with conventional VEGFR-targeted therapy but has shown enriched benefit from IO-based regimens), BAP1 loss of expression, and PD-L1 CPS of 14 on the 22C3 assay.

Her initial response was encouraging: after 4 induction cycles, restaging CT demonstrated a partial response in her pulmonary disease (right lower lobe dominant nodule shrank from 1.7 cm to 0.9 cm; other pulmonary nodules also decreased), though a new small right pelvic sidewall lymph node of 0.8 cm was noted and felt to be indeterminate at that time. She subsequently transitioned to nivolumab maintenance and was followed with interval labs and a clinical visit at the 6-month mark. At that visit she was tolerating therapy well, reporting only mild fatigue and grade 1 arthralgias of the bilateral wrists and fingers. Thyroid function was stable on levothyroxine, initiated for grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism that developed during cycle 4 of induction. She also experienced grade 2 immune-mediated dermatitis during cycle 3, managed with topical steroids without systemic immunosuppression or dose delay.

Her most recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, performed approximately one week ago, now reveals unequivocal radiographic progression. The right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has grown from 0.9 cm (nadir) back to 2.4 cm. The right upper lobe nodule has increased from 0.5 cm to 1.2 cm. The left lower lobe nodule has grown from 0.7 cm to 1.5 cm. Multiple new small bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 0.4 cm are now present, not seen on prior imaging. The previously indeterminate right pelvic sidewall lymph node has enlarged from 0.8 cm to 1.9 cm, and a new right external iliac lymph node measuring 1.4 cm has been identified. A dedicated MRI brain performed at the same time was negative for intracranial metastatic disease (stable mild white matter changes only). There is no hepatic or osseous disease on the CT.

In the context of this imaging progression, the patient reports a 4-6 week history of worsening exertional dyspnea. She previously walked several blocks without limitation; now she becomes noticeably short of breath after approximately one block on flat ground. She also reports a new dry, non-productive cough that began several weeks ago. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, pleuritic pain, fever, chills, or night sweats. No bone pain, headache, visual changes, or neurological symptoms. She has noticed mild worsening of her baseline fatigue but attributes this in part to worry and disrupted sleep since learning about the imaging findings.

She has been compliant with nivolumab infusions through today and denies any new symptoms that would suggest an active immune-related adverse event (no rash flare, no abdominal pain, no new diarrhea, no polyuria/polydipsia, no visual changes).

Goals of care discussion was briefly revisited on a phone call earlier this week. Patient confirmed she wishes to continue active systemic treatment with the goal of disease control and quality of life preservation. She understands this is not a curative situation but is motivated to remain on therapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Dyspnea on exertion (worsening over 4-6 weeks, now limiting at approximately 1 block), dry non-productive cough (new), mild-moderate fatigue, mild sleep disturbance related to anxiety about disease progression, intermittent grade 1 bilateral wrist and finger arthralgias (stable from prior visit, attributed to residual immune-related arthralgia).

Negative: No hemoptysis, no chest pain, no pleuritic symptoms, no wheezing, no fever, no chills, no night sweats, no headache, no vision changes, no confusion, no focal neurological deficits, no bone pain, no back pain, no abdominal pain, no nausea, no vomiting, no significant diarrhea, no blood in stool, no dysuria, no lower extremity edema, no new rash, no oral ulcers, no significant weight loss (weight essentially stable over the past 3 months, down approximately 1 kg total).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg (right arm, seated), heart rate 84 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 95% on room air (ambulatory; at rest 97% on room air), temperature 37.0 C, weight 78.2 kg, BMI 28.2.

General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing but mildly anxious. No acute distress at rest. Pleasant and engaged.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mucous membranes moist. No oral lesions, no thrush. Pupils equal and reactive.

Neck: Supple. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Mild decrease in breath sounds at the right lung base, without egophony or frank consolidation. No wheeze. Breathing slightly labored with ambulation from waiting room to examination room (desaturation to 94% transiently). Percussion note mildly dull at the right base.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed right flank incision from prior nephrectomy. No hepatosplenomegaly. No ascites. No palpable abdominal or pelvic mass.

Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or peripheral edema. No palmar-plantar erythema or blistering (no signs of hand-foot syndrome at this time, pre-cabozantinib).

Skin: No active rash. Prior grade 2 immune-related dermatitis has fully resolved. No areas of active erythema.

Musculoskeletal: Mild symmetric tenderness to palpation over bilateral metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. No frank synovitis. No limitation of range of motion.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait steady.

Performance Status: ECOG 1.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count: WBC 6.4 K/uL (differential normal, no eosinophilia), hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable from prior), hematocrit 34.8%, platelets 241 K/uL.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.55 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 44 mL/min/1.73m2 by CKD-EPI; mildly worsened from prior eGFR 48 but within recent baseline range), glucose 112 mg/dL (slightly elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes). Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, AST 31 U/L, ALT 36 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 82 U/L, albumin 3.8 g/dL.

Thyroid Function: TSH 2.6 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily).

HbA1c (checked 3 weeks ago): 7.2% (well-controlled).

Lipase: 28 U/L (normal).

LDH: 218 U/L (mildly elevated above the upper limit of normal at this institution, consistent with increased disease burden).

Urinalysis: Trace protein (1+), no casts, no hematuria. Consistent with baseline CKD.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (most recent, approximately 1 week ago): Reviewed in detail. Compared with the scan from 6 months ago, there is unambiguous growth and worsening of all target pulmonary nodules. The dominant right lower lobe mass has more than doubled in size back to 2.4 cm. New small bilateral pulmonary nodules have appeared. The previously indeterminate right pelvic sidewall lymph node is now clearly enlarged at 1.9 cm and represents confirmed nodal progression, with an additional new 1.4 cm right external iliac node. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases. No pleural effusion.

MRI Brain with and without Gadolinium (most recent, approximately 1 week ago): No intracranial metastatic disease. Stable mild white matter changes consistent with prior small vessel ischemic disease.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (pT3b pN2 M1, ISUP/WHO grade 4, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation) with radiographic progressive disease on first-line nivolumab maintenance after initial partial response to ipilimumab plus nivolumab induction. Total time on first-line IO therapy approximately 9 months. The progression pattern involves enlargement of existing pulmonary nodules, emergence of new small pulmonary nodules, and confirmed growth of pelvic and iliac nodal disease. No CNS or bone involvement.

   The patient is symptomatic from this progression, with worsening dyspnea on exertion and new dry cough, attributed to the increasing pulmonary tumor burden. ECOG PS remains 1. She is motivated to continue active systemic treatment.

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism - stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH within normal range. No further adjustment needed at this time.

3. Immune-related arthralgia (grade 1, residual) - mild bilateral small joint symptoms, not requiring treatment beyond watchful waiting. No new or escalating immune-mediated toxicity identified on review today.

4. Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (eGFR approximately 44, down from baseline eGFR 74 prior to nephrectomy) - stable over the past few months. Renal function has been impacted by prior radical nephrectomy, acute-on-chronic injury in the postoperative period, and likely ongoing low-grade nephrotoxicity from systemic therapy. Will need close monitoring with next-line therapy.

5. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - well controlled on metformin. HbA1c 7.2%. Blood glucose mildly elevated today, consistent with her baseline.

6. Hypertension - blood pressure 138/86 today, reasonably controlled on amlodipine 5 mg (note: she is pre-cabozantinib initiation; anticipate need for close BP monitoring on TKI therapy and possible dose adjustment).

---

PLAN

1. Discontinue ipilimumab/nivolumab. The patient has experienced clear radiographic and clinical progression after an initial partial response to dual checkpoint blockade. Further immunotherapy dosing is not anticipated to provide meaningful benefit at this point. Given the absence of active severe immune-related adverse events (no active colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, or endocrinopathy beyond well-managed hypothyroidism), she is appropriate to proceed directly to next-line therapy.

2. Initiate second-line cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg orally once daily with food. Rationale for cabozantinib selection:
   - Tumor NGS demonstrates high-level MET amplification (copy number 7.2), providing strong biologic rationale for cabozantinib over alternative VEGFR-only inhibitors (sunitinib, pazopanib), as cabozantinib simultaneously targets VEGFR2, MET, and AXL
   - Published data (METEOR trial) and real-world experience support second-line cabozantinib after IO failure
   - Her renal function (eGFR 44) is acceptable for TKI initiation; no dose reduction required at this time based solely on renal function
   - She has not received prior VEGFR-targeted therapy (IO/IO first-line), making her naive to this class

3. Toxicity counseling for cabozantinib completed in detail today:
   - Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Preventive measures discussed - urea-based emollient cream (e.g., Eucerin, CeraVe) twice daily to palms and soles starting day 1; well-cushioned footwear; avoid tight-fitting shoes; minimize trauma to hands and feet
   - Hypertension: Blood pressure logs requested; given pre-existing hypertension, we anticipate possible worsening and have a low threshold to uptitrate amlodipine or add a second agent; she understands to call if home BP readings are consistently above 150/95
   - Diarrhea: Loperamide to be taken at the first sign of loose stools; dietary modifications (BRAT-adjacent diet, low-fat, low-fiber); adequate hydration; call clinic if more than 4 stools above baseline per day or if dehydration develops
   - Fatigue: Expected; counsel on energy conservation, light aerobic activity as tolerated
   - Hepatotoxicity: LFTs to be checked at 4-week follow-up; she should report right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, or dark urine
   - Mucositis/stomatitis: Oral hygiene, avoid alcohol-based mouthwashes
   - Nausea: Take medication with a light snack if needed; antiemetics prescribed (ondansetron 4 mg ODT PRN)
   - Wound healing: Cabozantinib to be held at least 28 days prior to any elective surgery; no planned procedures at this time

4. Drug interactions reviewed: No significant interactions with metformin, levothyroxine, or amlodipine. Reviewed grapefruit/grapefruit juice avoidance (CYP3A4 consideration). Reviewed antacid/PPI timing (take cabozantinib at least 2 hours before or 2 hours after any proton pump inhibitors or antacids if needed). Patient does not currently use PPIs.

5. Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Repeat TFTs in 6-8 weeks.

6. Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily (anticipate possible dose increase given cabozantinib-related hypertension risk; close BP monitoring as above).

7. Follow-up labs in 4 weeks: CBC, CMP (including creatinine, LFTs), TSH, urinalysis. Phone or portal contact at 2 weeks to assess early toxicity (particularly hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea) and blood pressure.

8. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months (after approximately 12 weeks on cabozantinib).

9. Goals of care: Briefly discussed. Patient remains committed to active treatment. She has a strong family support system and has been engaged with the oncology social worker. I introduced the concept of palliative care as a complementary service focused on symptom management and quality of life - she expressed openness to a future referral but declines formal referral today. We will revisit this at the next visit.

10. Clinical trial: Reviewed current open trials at our institution. There are no trials currently enrolling for second-line or later RCC that this patient would qualify for. We will continue to monitor for new trial openings and the patient understands she may be re-evaluated for trial eligibility at the time of any future progression.

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan. Written materials on cabozantinib provided. She has contact information for the triage nurse line and understands which symptoms warrant urgent communication. She will follow up in 4 weeks or sooner if needed.

Physician: Attending Medical Oncologist
Time spent: 45 minutes (greater than 50% in counseling and coordination of care)
